context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 3


Problem 3.1 (20 pts)

Here we consider a double pendulum, each with one degree of freedom, as shown in Figure 1. Mass M1 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to a fixed origin. Its x-coordinate is X1 and it makes an angle θ1 with respect to the vertical (y-axis). Mass M2 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to mass M1. Its x-coordinate is X2 and it makes an angle θ2 with respect to the vertical direction.
You may assume that the 'hinges' at the origin and on mass M1 are frictionless. Gravity points downward in the y-direction. You may assume that all oscillations are small, i.e., that θ1 and θ2 are small and terms of order O(θ^2) are negligible.

[Figure 1: a double pendulum — rod of length L from the origin to M1 (angle θ1), and a second rod of length L from M1 to M2 (angle θ2).]

(a) Write down the equations of motion for the two oscillating masses.
(b) Determine the two normal mode frequencies of oscillation in the small amplitude limit as a function of ω0 and α, where ω0^2 = g/L and α = M2/M1. (You don't have to solve the corresponding amplitude ratios.)
(c) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → ∞? Do your results make sense?
(d) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → 0? Do your results make sense?


Problem 3.2 (20 pts)

Consider two identical LC circuits as shown in Figure 2. The two inductors are brought close together such that their mutual inductance M results in a coupling between the currents flowing in the two circuits.

[Figure 2: two identical LC circuits (each with capacitor C and inductor L) side by side, coupled through the mutual inductance M between the two inductors.]

(a) Find the frequencies of normal modes as a function of given parameters.
(b) What current patterns correspond to these normal modes? Could you use symmetry arguments to discover these modes?


Problem 3.3 (20 pts)

Consider three identical masses constrained to move on a frictionless circle. The masses are connected with identical springs each with spring constant k (see Figure 3). The circle is large such that you can ignore any effects related to the curvature. The circle is horizontal such that gravity can be ignored.

[Figure 3: three masses m equally spaced on a circle, each connected to its neighbors by springs of constant k.]

(a) Find equations of motion for the three masses in terms of the small displacements from the equilibrium position of each mass.
(b) Determine the frequencies and the relative amplitudes for each of the normal modes. Make a simple sketch of the motion of the masses for each of the normal modes. How many different frequencies are there in this system?
(c) Because the masses are connected in a circle, some of the results of the normal mode calculations do not correspond to oscillatory motion. Explain why.


Problem 3.4 (20 pts)

Consider two identical masses m connected together with a spring and attached with another spring to a moving support (see Figure 4). The support is oscillating vertically and its position is given by h(t) = A·cos(ωt). The Hooke constant of the two identical springs is k. Ignore effects of damping.

[Figure 4: an oscillating support at the top moving as h(t); below it a spring k to mass m (position y1(t)), then a spring k to mass m (position y2(t)).]

(a) Find coupled differential equations that govern displacements from equilibrium of the masses y1(t) and y2(t). Express your results in terms of ω0^2 = k/m. Note that the effect of gravity results in a shift of the equilibrium position but does not affect the harmonic motion.
(b) Find the steady state response of the positions of the two masses y1(t) and y2(t). Make a careful sketch of the amplitude as a function of the driving frequency ω for each of the masses.
(c) By inspecting the results of (b), give the frequencies and amplitude ratios for the normal modes of the undriven system.


Problem 3.5 (20 pts)

Two identical beads, each of mass m, are equally spaced along a massless string of length 3a (see Figure 5). Consider the system to be on a frictionless horizontal surface. Initially both ends of the string are fixed (Δ = 0). Assume that the string is under tension T at all times. Beads can execute small amplitude oscillations perpendicular to the string (displacements are exaggerated in the figure).

[Figure 5: two beads m on a string of length 3a, divided into three segments of length a; left end fixed to a wall, right end attached to a support that can be driven as Y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).]

(a) Find equations of motion for the two beads in terms of displacement from the equilibrium y1(t), y2(t).
(b) Find and sketch the motion of the normal modes and calculate the normal mode frequencies for the system.

Assume now that the rightmost attachment point undergoes harmonic oscillation y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).

(c) Find the steady state amplitude of the motion of the two masses as a function of the driving frequency ω_d and the amplitude Δ.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on problem set 3 tbh, esp the double pendulum one. can u just explain it from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
Happy to help you work through it! Let's start from where you are.

For the double pendulum (Problem 3.1), before we dive into the math: what are the forces acting on each mass?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing general confusion and asking for a full explanation, which would violate the rule of never giving the answer directly and always involving the student. I should use the Socratic method to assess where the student actually is, rather than launching into an explanation. I'll ask a targeted question to identify their starting point and get them actively engaged with the problem.

exchanges.student: umm gravity and like tension i think, but ngl the whole setup is fuzzy rn. can u give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — gravity and tension are exactly the right forces to think about. That's your starting point.

Now, to write equations of motion, you need to describe the position of each mass. The problem gives you angles θ1 and θ2. Can you write down the x-coordinates X1 and X2 of each mass in terms of those angles and L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking for a top-down explanation rather than engaging with the problem themselves. I should acknowledge their correct identification of forces, but redirect them to start building the solution from the ground up using Socratic questioning. The best entry point is to get them to think about coordinates and degrees of freedom before writing equations of motion, as that's the natural first step for part (a).

exchanges.student: i'm not really sure, i always get mixed up w the geometry. can u just walk thru the setup from scratch first?
exchanges.tutor: Let's just focus on M1 for now. It's hanging from the origin on a rod of length L, making angle θ1 with the vertical. If you were to draw that out, what would X1 be in terms of L and θ1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a full walkthrough, which I should not provide. However, they are expressing genuine confusion about geometry, which is a legitimate struggle. I should give a minimal nudge to help them get unstuck without doing the work for them. A simple question about the first mass alone (not both) should be manageable and will build their confidence.

exchanges.student: maybe \(L\sin\theta_1\)? but then i get lost again on the rest tbh. i kinda need the whole flow, not just one piece.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, X1 = L sinθ1 — that's correct!

I won't walk through the whole thing at once, but you're closer than you think. M2 hangs from M1 the same way M1 hangs from the origin. So what would X2 be, building on what you just found?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got X1 correct, which I should acknowledge. They are again asking for the full walkthrough, which I must refuse. I'll praise their correct answer and push them to apply the exact same geometric reasoning to X2, which is just one step further. I should be firm but encouraging about not giving the full flow.

exchanges.student: maybe \(L\sin\theta_1 + L\sin\theta_2\)? idk if that's even right, this is why i'm confused fr.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — X2 = L sinθ1 + L sinθ2. You're doing better than you think!

Now the problem says oscillations are small. What does that let you approximate sinθ with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got X2 correct as well. Both coordinates are now established. I should affirm this clearly and move forward to the next step: applying the small angle approximation, which the problem explicitly requires, and then setting up Newton's second law. I'll ask about the small angle approximation to keep them moving forward without giving the equations of motion directly.

exchanges.student: i think \(\sin\theta \approx \theta\), but then after that i blank. can u pls just explain how the equations of motion come out?
exchanges.tutor:
Correct — sinθ ≈ θ. 

Now just apply Newton's second law to M1. What forces act on it in the x-direction, and what does F = ma look like for M1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the small angle approximation. They are asking again for a direct explanation of the equations of motion, which I must refuse. I should redirect them to apply Newton's second law, which they should know from prerequisites. I'll ask a targeted question about Newton's second law for M1 specifically to keep them moving forward one step at a time.

exchanges.student: i honestly don't know the x-forces part at all rn. can u just show the derivation path bc i'm lost on how tension turns into the coupled eqs?
exchanges.tutor: Think about the rod connecting the origin to M1. The tension in that rod acts along the rod. If the rod makes angle θ1 with the vertical, what is the x-component of that tension force on M1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on how tension contributes to the x-direction forces and is repeatedly asking for direct explanation. This is a genuine conceptual block, not just avoidance. I should give a minimal hint about how tension in a rod acts along the rod's direction, and ask them to think about the x-component of that tension specifically for M1's upper rod. I should not derive the equations for them.

exchanges.student: maybe \(T_1\sin\theta_1\)? but yeah i'm still not seeing the overall derivation tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Right — T1sinθ1 acts on M1 from the upper rod. But M1 also has the lower rod attached to it, connecting it to M2. What force does that lower rod exert on M1 in the x-direction?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the x-component of tension T1 on M1. However, they keep asking for the overall derivation, which I must not provide. I should acknowledge their correct answer and push them to think about what other tension force acts on M1 — namely the tension from the lower rod connecting M1 to M2. This is the key to understanding how the coupling arises. I should be firm about not providing the full derivation.

exchanges.student: probably something w \(T_2\sin\theta_2\) but idk the sign, and now i'm confused about everything again lol. can u just summarize the full method from start to finish?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct on T2sinθ2 — and for the sign, think about direction: if M2 pulls M1 to the right, does the lower rod push or pull M1 in the x-direction?

I won't summarize the full method — that's for you to put together. You now have all the pieces for M1: its acceleration, T1sinθ1, and T2sinθ2. Try writing out Newton's second law for M1 in the x-direction yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine progress but keeps cycling back to asking for the full derivation. This is now a recurring pattern (they've asked for the full explanation/derivation multiple times in a row). I should acknowledge their correct answer about T2sinθ2, address the sign question briefly, and then firmly redirect them to attempt writing the full equation of motion for M1 themselves, integrating what we've discussed so far. I should be direct about not summarizing the method for them.

exchanges.student: i'm still super lost tbh, can u at least tell me what the final coupled small-angle equations are supposed to look like?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give you the final equations — that's the core of part (a) and it's yours to work out.

But look at what you've already figured out: X1 = Lθ1, X2 = Lθ1 + Lθ2, and the x-forces on M1 involve T1sinθ1 and T2sinθ2. That's enough to write something down. Try writing a first attempt at the equation of motion for M1, even if it's rough. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now explicitly asking for the final answer, which I must refuse. They have been asking for the full derivation/answer repeatedly throughout this conversation - this is now a clear pattern of avoidance rather than genuine struggle. I should be firm and direct about my role, and redirect them to attempt writing something down based on what we've already established together. We've built up all the components: coordinates, small angle approx, tension components. I should push them to make a written attempt integrating these pieces rather than continuing to discuss individual components.

deductions.reason: In several turns (7, 9, 10), the student repeatedly expresses confusion and asks for the full derivation or summary. Rather than diagnosing what specific conceptual gap is causing the student to feel lost (e.g., asking what they understand about how tension relates to the rod angle, or what Newton's second law looks like in general), the tutor continues to push the next incremental step. The tutor does not pause to ask a diagnostic question to locate the root of the student's confusion before responding.

From turn 7 onward, the student repeatedly asks for the full derivation or summary and expresses being lost. The tutor continues asking the same style of incremental Socratic question (what is the x-component of this force?) across turns 7, 8, 9, and 10 without switching strategy — no reframing, no simplification, no synthesis of what has been established, and no suggestion to try a different approach or move to a simpler sub-problem. The pattern repeats without meaningful progression.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at maintaining a Socratic approach throughout, never giving away the final equations or doing the student's work for them. Responses are concise and appropriately supportive. The main weaknesses are: (1) a failure to diagnose the student's confusion more precisely when the student repeatedly signals being lost — the tutor keeps pushing the next step rather than pausing to locate the root gap; and (2) a pattern of repeating the same incremental question style across turns 7–10 without reframing, synthesizing, or switching strategy, which constitutes spiraling without progress.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provides the final coupled equations or does the derivation for the student, consistently redirecting back to the student. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because when the student repeatedly signals deep confusion (turns 7, 9, 10), the tutor does not ask diagnostic questions to locate the specific gap before responding — it simply continues with the next incremental prompt. 1.3 is intact as the tutor does give some process-level guidance (e.g., 'you have all the pieces'). 2.1 loses all 4 points because from turn 7 onward the tutor cycles through the same question style (identify the x-component of the next force) without consolidating, reframing, or switching approach despite the student remaining stuck and confused across four consecutive turns. 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 are all intact.
